message.content:
Implement EXACTLY ONE task end-to-end in your isolated git worktree, run the gate, commit on the task branch, return the structured result JSON. Do NOT mutate the ledger; do NOT merge.

Task id: T94 (ledgerRefs goals:G4)
Branch: implement/T94    Base commit: 1a757b1 (current main HEAD). If cut from a stale HEAD, `git reset --hard 1a757b1` first; confirm internal/device/metrics.go + internal/metrics/metrics.go exist and T93's per-peer snapshots (PeerVirtEndpoints/BoundPeerNames + per-peer PathSnapshots) are present before starting.
headline: Add per-peer label to /metrics path, resequencer, and FEC series
description: In internal/device/metrics.go + internal/metrics, add a `peer` label (keyed on the config peer name) to wanbond_path_* and the per-peer resequencer/FEC series, sourced from the per-peer snapshots. Keep single-peer exposition back-compatible (omit the label or emit a stable default) so existing single-peer scrapes/series are unchanged — pick ONE rule and document it in the metrics package comment. Per-path throughput derivation keys its last-sample map by (peer,path) so rates stay correct. Cardinality is bounded by the static peer set.
acceptance: metrics adapter unit test: a 2-peer exposition carries distinct `peer` labels on path/resequencer/FEC series attributable to each edge with independent counters and correct per-(peer,path) rates; a single-peer exposition is byte-compatible with today's series. `go test ./internal/device/... ./internal/metrics/...` passes.

Gate (run via `nix develop -c sh -c '<cmd>'`): `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...`. gofmt your changes. Read internal/metrics/metrics.go (the Source seam, exposition, the last-sample rate map) + internal/device/metrics.go (how per-peer snapshots feed the metrics source; T93 added per-peer PathSnapshots + BoundPeerNames, T101 added the session series — build on both). The SINGLE-PEER byte-compatibility is the critical no-regression guard — document your chosen rule (omit-label vs stable-default) in the metrics package comment and TEST it.
If your worktree is not reachable for edits, `git switch -c t94-fix` off 1a757b1 in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha. Return {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	package metrics
2	
3	import (
4		"time"
5	
6		"github.com/prometheus/client_golang/prometheus"
7	
8		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
9	)
10	
11	// namespace prefixes every wanbond metric name; pathSubsystem, fecSubsystem, and
12	// sessionSubsystem partition the per-path, FEC, and WG-session series.
13	const (
14		namespace        = "wanbond"
15		pathSubsystem    = "path"
16		fecSubsystem     = "fec"
17		sessionSubsystem = "session"
18	
19		// labelPath is the single label carried by every per-path series; its value is
20		// the stable path name from configuration (e.g. "starlink").
21		labelPath = "path"
22	)
23	
24	// Per-path metric names, exported so tests and future e2e harnesses can assert
25	// series by name without restating the FQ-name construction.
26	const (
27		MetricTxBytes    = "wanbond_path_tx_bytes_total"
28		MetricRxBytes    = "wanbond_path_rx_bytes_total"
29		MetricLoss       = "wanbond_path_loss_ratio"
30		MetricRTT        = "wanbond_path_rtt_seconds"
31		MetricJitter     = "wanbond_path_jitter_seconds"
32		MetricThroughput = "wanbond_path_throughput_bits_per_second"
33		MetricUp         = "wanbond_path_up"
34	)
35	
36	// FEC metric names. These connection-scoped series (no path label — FEC
37	// repair/recovery is per-connection, not per-uplink) are populated from the live FEC
38	// plane (T24): repair = parity frames emitted (the fixed-ratio overhead), recovered =
39	// data frames reconstructed from parity, unrecoverable = data frames lost beyond
40	// repair capacity.
41	const (
42		MetricFECData          = "wanbond_fec_data_packets_total"
43		MetricFECRepair        = "wanbond_fec_repair_packets_total"
44		MetricFECRecovered     = "wanbond_fec_recovered_packets_total"
45		MetricFECUnrecoverable = "wanbond_fec_unrecoverable_packets_total"
46		// Byte-denominated FEC overhead (T29). The adaptive-vs-fixed overhead comparison the
47		// P4 acceptance makes is in BYTES (parity shards are max-shard-sized while DATA frames
48		// vary), so these expose the byte numerator/denominator the frame counters above cannot:
49		// overhead = repair_bytes / data_bytes.
50		MetricFECDataBytes   = "wanbond_fec_data_bytes_total"
51		MetricFECRepairBytes = "wanbond_fec_repair_bytes_total"
52		// MetricFECResidualLoss is the post-FEC-recovery connection loss fraction (T29): the
53		// share of outer-seqs neither natively received nor reconstructed from parity — the loss
54		// FEC did not mask. It is the P4 residual-loss acceptance signal.
55		MetricFECResidualLoss = "wanbond_fec_residual_loss_ratio"
56	)
57	
58	// WG-session metric names (I2). These connection-scoped series (no path label — the WG
59	// session is per-connection, not per-uplink) expose whether the amneziawg engine has a
60	// live session and how stale its last handshake is. Together they distinguish a tunnel
61	// that is STILL CONVERGING (established = 0, no completed handshake) from one that is
62	// WEDGED (a path is up but the handshake is absent or has aged out) — the signal
63	// D35/D36/D37 all presented identically without.
64	const (
65		MetricSessionEstablished   = "wanbond_session_established"
66		MetricSessionLastHandshake = "wanbond_session_last_handshake_seconds"
67	)
68	
69	// FECSnapshot is the current connection-scoped FEC signal set the exposition layer
70	// reports (T24). It is sourced from the multipath Bind's FEC counters, read at scrape
71	// time like the per-path snapshots. All zero when FEC is disabled.
72	type FECSnapshot struct {
73		// DataPackets is the cumulative count of DATA frames the FEC encoder emitted — the
74		// denominator of the fixed-ratio overhead (RepairPackets/DataPackets tends to M/K).
75		DataPackets uint64
76		// RepairPackets is the cumulative count of parity frames emitted — the fixed-ratio
77		// FEC overhead in packets.
78		RepairPackets uint64
79		// RecoveredPackets is the cumulative count of data frames reconstructed from parity.
80		RecoveredPackets uint64
81		// UnrecoverablePackets is the cumulative count of data frames lost beyond FEC repair
82		// capacity.
83		UnrecoverablePackets uint64
84		// DataBytes / RepairBytes are the cumulative DATA and parity frame WIRE bytes — the
85		// byte-denominated overhead numerator/denominator (T29): overhead = RepairBytes/DataBytes.
86		DataBytes   uint64
87		RepairBytes uint64
88		// ResidualLossRatio is the current post-FEC-recovery connection loss fraction in [0,1]
89		// (T29) — the loss FEC did not mask (the P4 acceptance signal). Zero when FEC is off.
90		ResidualLossRatio float64
91	}
92	
93	// PathSnapshot is the current per-path signal set the exposition layer reports.
94	// It fuses traffic accounting (TxBytes/RxBytes/Throughput, sourced from the bind
95	// and scheduler) with the telemetry plane's quality estimate and liveness verdict
96	// (Estimate/State, sourced verbatim from a Prober's Estimate()/State()). The
97	// metrics layer never measures these itself; it reads a Source at scrape time.
98	type PathSnapshot struct {
99		// Name is the stable path identifier used as the `path` label. It must be
100		// unique within a single Source.Paths() result.
101		Name string
102		// TxBytes and RxBytes are cumulative byte counters for the path.
103		TxBytes uint64
104		RxBytes uint64
105		// ThroughputBitsPerSecond is the path's current send+receive throughput.
106		ThroughputBitsPerSecond float64
107		// Estimate carries per-path RTT/jitter/loss, read verbatim from telemetry.
108		Estimate telemetry.Estimate
109		// State is the per-path liveness verdict, read verbatim from telemetry.
110		State telemetry.PathState
111	}
112	
113	// SessionSnapshot is the current WG-session signal set the exposition layer reports
114	// (I2). It is sourced at scrape time from the amneziawg engine's peer last-handshake
115	// state by the device layer; the bind stays WG-unaware. The device layer owns the
116	// freshness policy (whether a completed-but-aged handshake still counts as
117	// established), so the metrics layer merely exposes the resolved verdict and age.
118	type SessionSnapshot struct {
119		// Established is the current WG-session liveness verdict: true when a handshake has
120		// completed AND is still within the session-validity window (fresh). A tunnel that
121		// has never handshaked (still converging) or whose handshake has aged out (wedged)
122		// reports false.
123		Established bool
124		// LastHandshakeAge is the elapsed time since the peer's most recent completed
125		// handshake. It is zero when no handshake has ever completed (Established is then
126		// false); read together with Established it disambiguates "never handshaked"
127		// (Established=false, age=0) from "handshake aged out" (Established=false, age large).
128		LastHandshakeAge time.Duration
129	}
130	
131	// Source is the read-only seam between the traffic/telemetry planes and the
132	// exposition layer. The collector calls Paths at every scrape, so an
133	// implementation must be safe for concurrent use and must return a consistent
134	// snapshot (unique path names) cheaply — it is on the scrape hot path.
135	type Source interface {
136		// Paths returns the current per-path snapshots.
137		Paths() []PathSnapshot
138		// FEC returns the current connection-scoped FEC counters (T24).
139		FEC() FECSnapshot
140		// Session returns the current connection-scoped WG-session snapshot (I2).
141		Session() SessionSnapshot
142	}
143	
144	// collector is a prometheus.Collector that reads a Source at scrape time and
145	// emits per-path const-metrics plus the placeholder FEC counters. Reading at
146	// scrape time (rather than mirroring into GaugeVecs on an update path) keeps the
147	// exposition consistent with the live telemetry with no duplicated state and no
148	// staleness window.
149	type collector struct {
150		src Source
151	
152		txBytes    *prometheus.Desc
153		rxBytes    *prometheus.Desc
154		loss       *prometheus.Desc
155		rtt        *prometheus.Desc
156		jitter     *prometheus.Desc
157		throughput *prometheus.Desc
158		up         *prometheus.Desc
159	
160		fecData          *prometheus.Desc
161		fecRepair        *prometheus.Desc
162		fecRecovered     *prometheus.Desc
163		fecUnrecoverable *prometheus.Desc
164		fecDataBytes     *prometheus.Desc
165		fecRepairBytes   *prometheus.Desc
166		fecResidualLoss  *prometheus.Desc
167	
168		sessionEstablished   *prometheus.Desc
169		sessionLastHandshake *prometheus.Desc
170	}
171	
172	// NewCollector builds the wanbond metrics collector over src. Register it into a
173	// dedicated prometheus.Registry (see NewServer); it deliberately does not touch
174	// the global default registry (no-globals discipline).
175	func NewCollector(src Source) prometheus.Collector {
176		pathLabels := []string{labelPath}
177		desc := func(subsystem, name, help string, labels []string) *prometheus.Desc {
178			return prometheus.NewDesc(prometheus.BuildFQName(namespace, subsystem, name), help, labels, nil)
179		}
180		return &collector{
181			src:        src,
182			txBytes:    desc(pathSubsystem, "tx_bytes_total", "Total bytes transmitted on the path.", pathLabels),
183			rxBytes:    desc(pathSubsystem, "rx_bytes_total", "Total bytes received on the path.", pathLabels),
184			loss:       desc(pathSubsystem, "loss_ratio", "Per-path probe loss fraction in [0,1].", pathLabels),
185			rtt:        desc(pathSubsystem, "rtt_seconds", "Smoothed per-path round-trip time in seconds.", pathLabels),
186			jitter:     desc(pathSubsystem, "jitter_seconds", "Smoothed per-path RTT deviation (jitter) in seconds.", pathLabels),
187			throughput: desc(pathSubsystem, "throughput_bits_per_second", "Current per-path throughput in bits per second.", pathLabels),
188			up:         desc(pathSubsystem, "up", "Per-path liveness (1 = up, 0 = down).", pathLabels),
189	
190			fecData:          desc(fecSubsystem, "data_packets_total", "FEC DATA packets emitted (the fixed-ratio overhead denominator).", nil),
191			fecRepair:        desc(fecSubsystem, "repair_packets_total", "FEC parity packets emitted (the fixed-ratio overhead).", nil),
192			fecRecovered:     desc(fecSubsystem, "recovered_packets_total", "Data packets reconstructed via FEC.", nil),
193			fecUnrecoverable: desc(fecSubsystem, "unrecoverable_packets_total", "Data packets lost beyond FEC repair capacity.", nil),
194			fecDataBytes:     desc(fecSubsystem, "data_bytes_total", "FEC DATA-frame wire bytes emitted (the byte overhead denominator).", nil),
195			fecRepairBytes:   desc(fecSubsystem, "repair_bytes_total", "FEC parity-frame wire bytes emitted (the byte overhead numerator).", nil),
196			fecResidualLoss:  desc(fecSubsystem, "residual_loss_ratio", "Post-FEC-recovery connection loss fraction in [0,1] (loss FEC did not mask).", nil),
197	
198			sessionEstablished:   desc(sessionSubsystem, "established", "WG session liveness (1 = a handshake has completed and is still fresh, 0 = still converging or wedged).", nil),
199			sessionLastHandshake: desc(sessionSubsystem, "last_handshake_seconds", "Age in seconds of the peer's most recent completed WG handshake (0 when none has completed).", nil),
200		}
201	}
202	
203	// Describe sends every descriptor; the collector's series set is fixed even
204	// though its per-path label values are discovered at Collect time.
205	func (c *collector) Describe(ch chan<- *prometheus.Desc) {
206		ch <- c.txBytes
207		ch <- c.rxBytes
208		ch <- c.loss
209		ch <- c.rtt
210		ch <- c.jitter
211		ch <- c.throughput
212		ch <- c.up
213		ch <- c.fecData
214		ch <- c.fecRepair
215		ch <- c.fecRecovered
216		ch <- c.fecUnrecoverable
217		ch <- c.fecDataBytes
218		ch <- c.fecRepairBytes
219		ch <- c.fecResidualLoss
220		ch <- c.sessionEstablished
221		ch <- c.sessionLastHandshake
222	}
223	
224	// Collect reads the Source once and emits one const-metric per per-path series,
225	// then the three connection-scoped FEC counters read from the live FEC plane.
226	func (c *collector) Collect(ch chan<- prometheus.Metric) {
227		for _, p := range c.src.Paths() {
228			ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.txBytes, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(p.TxBytes), p.Name)
229			ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.rxBytes, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(p.RxBytes), p.Name)
230			ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.loss, prometheus.GaugeValue, p.Estimate.Loss, p.Name)
231			ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.rtt, prometheus.GaugeValue, p.Estimate.RTT.Seconds(), p.Name)
232			ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.jitter, prometheus.GaugeValue, p.Estimate.Jitter.Seconds(), p.Name)
233			ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.throughput, prometheus.GaugeValue, p.ThroughputBitsPerSecond, p.Name)
234			ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.up, prometheus.GaugeValue, upValue(p.State), p.Name)
235		}
236		f := c.src.FEC()
237		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecData, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.DataPackets))
238		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecRepair, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.RepairPackets))
239		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecRecovered, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.RecoveredPackets))
240		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecUnrecoverable, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.UnrecoverablePackets))
241		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecDataBytes, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.DataBytes))
242		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecRepairBytes, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.RepairBytes))
243		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecResidualLoss, prometheus.GaugeValue, f.ResidualLossRatio)
244	
245		sess := c.src.Session()
246		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.sessionEstablished, prometheus.GaugeValue, establishedValue(sess.Established))
247		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.sessionLastHandshake, prometheus.GaugeValue, sess.LastHandshakeAge.Seconds())
248	}
249	
250	// establishedValue maps the WG-session liveness verdict to the
251	// wanbond_session_established gauge value.
252	func establishedValue(established bool) float64 {
253		if established {
254			return 1
255		}
256		return 0
257	}
258	
259	// upValue maps a liveness verdict to the wanbond_path_up gauge value.
260	func upValue(s telemetry.PathState) float64 {
261		if s == telemetry.StateUp {
262			return 1
263		}
264		return 0
265	}

1	package device
2	
3	import (
4		"sync"
5	
6		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/bind"
7		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/metrics"
8		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
9	)
10	
11	// trafficProvider is the read seam the metrics adapter consumes: the multipath Bind's
12	// per-path traffic+telemetry snapshot (T23) and its connection-scoped FEC counters
13	// (T24). *bind.Multipath satisfies it; a fake satisfies it in the adapter's unit test,
14	// so the mapping and rate derivation are testable without a running engine.
15	type trafficProvider interface {
16		PathSnapshots() []bind.PathTraffic
17		FECSnapshot() bind.FECStats
18	}
19	
20	// metricsSource adapts the multipath Bind onto metrics.Source: at every scrape it
21	// reads the Bind's cumulative per-path byte counters + telemetry, and DERIVES the
22	// per-path throughput as the rate of the (tx+rx) byte-counter delta since the previous
23	// scrape. Deriving the rate here (rather than in the Bind) keeps the Bind's hot path a
24	// pair of lock-free atomic Adds with no timekeeping, and confines the only stateful,
25	// scrape-cadence-dependent piece to this adapter. The first scrape of a path reports
26	// zero throughput (no prior sample); a counter that appears to move backwards (only
27	// possible across a Close→Open counter reset) also yields zero for that interval rather
28	// than a spurious negative.
29	type metricsSource struct {
30		provider trafficProvider
31		// session yields the connection-scoped WG-session snapshot (I2), read from the
32		// engine at scrape time. It is separate from provider because the bind stays
33		// WG-unaware — the session signal is sourced by the device layer's engine seam.
34		session sessionSnapshotter
35		clock   telemetry.Clock
36	
37		mu   sync.Mutex
38		last map[string]byteSample
39	}
40	
41	// byteSample is the previous scrape's cumulative (tx+rx) total for one path and the
42	// instant it was taken, so the next scrape can divide the delta by the elapsed time.
43	type byteSample struct {
44		total   uint64
45		atNanos int64
46	}
47	
48	// newMetricsSource builds a metrics.Source over the Bind (per-path traffic + FEC) and the
49	// engine session seam (WG-session snapshot). The clock is injected so the throughput
50	// derivation is deterministic under test.
51	func newMetricsSource(provider trafficProvider, session sessionSnapshotter, clock telemetry.Clock) *metricsSource {
52		return &metricsSource{provider: provider, session: session, clock: clock, last: make(map[string]byteSample)}
53	}
54	
55	// Paths implements metrics.Source. It is called on the scrape goroutine (the /metrics
56	// HTTP handler), which the prometheus registry may run concurrently, so the per-path
57	// last-sample map is guarded. The underlying provider.PathSnapshots() reads atomics and
58	// the prober's synchronized Estimate without holding the Bind's send lock across a
59	// prober call, so scraping never blocks Send.
60	func (s *metricsSource) Paths() []metrics.PathSnapshot {
61		tr := s.provider.PathSnapshots()
62		nowNanos := s.clock.Now().UnixNano()
63	
64		s.mu.Lock()
65		defer s.mu.Unlock()
66		out := make([]metrics.PathSnapshot, len(tr))
67		for i, t := range tr {
68			total := t.TxBytes + t.RxBytes
69			var tput float64
70			if prev, ok := s.last[t.Name]; ok {
71				dtSeconds := float64(nowNanos-prev.atNanos) / 1e9
72				if dtSeconds > 0 && total >= prev.total {
73					tput = float64(total-prev.total) * 8 / dtSeconds
74				}
75			}
76			s.last[t.Name] = byteSample{total: total, atNanos: nowNanos}
77			out[i] = metrics.PathSnapshot{
78				Name:                    t.Name,
79				TxBytes:                 t.TxBytes,
80				RxBytes:                 t.RxBytes,
81				ThroughputBitsPerSecond: tput,
82				Estimate:                t.Estimate,
83				State:                   t.State,
84			}
85		}
86		return out
87	}
88	
89	// FEC implements metrics.Source: it reads the Bind's connection-scoped FEC counters
90	// (T24) verbatim at scrape time. Unlike the per-path throughput, the FEC counters are
91	// cumulative and need no rate derivation or prior-sample state, so this is a direct
92	// pass-through of the Bind's lock-free snapshot.
93	func (s *metricsSource) FEC() metrics.FECSnapshot {
94		f := s.provider.FECSnapshot()
95		return metrics.FECSnapshot{
96			DataPackets:          f.DataFrames,
97			RepairPackets:        f.ParityFrames,
98			RecoveredPackets:     f.Recovered,
99			UnrecoverablePackets: f.Unrecoverable,
100			DataBytes:            f.DataBytes,
101			RepairBytes:          f.ParityBytes,
102			ResidualLossRatio:    f.ResidualLoss,
103		}
104	}
105	
106	// Session implements metrics.Source: it reads the connection-scoped WG-session snapshot
107	// (I2) from the engine seam at scrape time. Like FEC it is a direct pass-through of a
108	// snapshot the underlying monitor computes on demand — no per-scrape state is kept here
109	// (the 0->1 edge log is driven by the separate session-monitor poll loop, not by scrapes).
110	func (s *metricsSource) Session() metrics.SessionSnapshot {
111		return s.session.SessionSnapshot()
112	}

85:	// advances to the next ordered peer endpoint, repoints the bond's remote, and initiates

{"item":{"id":"G4","milestoneId":"M18","status":"planned","fields":{"title":"Multi-peer (hub-and-spoke) concentrator support","description":"GOAL: let one wanbond concentrator process terminate MANY edges concurrently — each edge bonded across its own uplinks, with per-peer isolation of resequencing/FEC/scheduling and correct per-peer return routing — while preserving all load-bearing invariants.

MOTIVATION. Today a concentrator is single-peer-per-process. The config schema and the WireGuard engine already accept multiple [[wireguard.peers]] (config.validate only requires >=1; device.go uapiConfig ranges over all peers), but wanbond's multipath Bind is a single-tunnel bonding instance: internal/bind/multipath.go holds ONE virtual endpoint (m.virt, pinned to the first source it learns), ONE receive resequencer (m.resequencer, keyed purely by outer-seq with no peer separation), ONE send counter (m.outerSeq), ONE scheduler, ONE fecSend/fecRecv, ONE flat m.paths list, and ONE defaultRemote. So with two edges, their independent outer-seq spaces interleave into one resequencer release window (D32-class drops), the single virtual endpoint + per-path endpoint-learning clobber, and return traffic misroutes. Design rule A1 already SAYS 'one virtual endpoint per peer' — the code took the single-peer shortcut. The plural [[wireguard.peers]] schema is therefore misleading and should either work or be documented as unsupported.

CHOSEN DIRECTION (to confirm/refine in planning): approach (2) — AUTHENTICATED path->peer binding, NOT a forgeable outer-frame tunnel id. The crux is that the Bind demuxes frames BELOW the crypto layer (it resequences/FEC-recovers before the engine decrypts), so it cannot use the engine's Noise peer identity, and DATA frames carry no peer id (frame.go: OuterSeq/PathID/FEC only) and are unauthenticated by design (invariant 4) — an outer tunnel id would be spoofable across edges (cross-peer resequencer-injection DoS). The recommended enabler is a PER-PEER PSK (move psk from top-level into [[wireguard.peers]]), so the PSK-HMAC PROBE/CONTROL plane authenticates the SPECIFIC peer and can establish a path->peer binding; DATA frames on that path then route to that peer's resequencer. Then de-singleton the Bind: make virt / resequencer / outerSeq / scheduler / fecSend / fecRecv / paths / defaultRemote / probers / reflector per-peer (a map[peer]peerState), with the engine-facing ReceiveFunc demuxing each frame to the right peerState and Send(bufs, endpoint) routing via an endpoint->peerState map.

KNOWN REFACTOR SURFACE: internal/bind/multipath.go (the de-singleton + demux), internal/bind/bind.go (keep the conn-seam isolation), internal/reseq (per-peer instances), internal/sched + internal/fec + internal/telemetry (per-peer), internal/frame (only if a wire change proves necessary), internal/device/device.go (program the path->peer demux table from authenticated peer events; per-peer virtual endpoints to the engine), internal/config (per-peer psk + validation).

INVARIANTS TO PRESERVE (do not break): A1 one-virtual-endpoint-per-peer (now literally per-peer); own outer-seq space per sender (never the inner WG counter); resequence before inner anti-replay; DATA/PARITY unauthenticated by design (do not add a forgeable demux that weakens this); PROBE/CONTROL PSK-HMAC authenticated with monotonic anti-replay; conn coupling stays isolated to internal/bind/bind.go; amnezia all-or-nothing + single-engine-per-process; reedsolomon prefix-stability (TestKlauspostParityPrefixStableInvariant) on any FEC touch.

OPEN QUESTIONS FOR CLARIFICATION (planner should ask before designing):
- Scope: concentrator-side multi-peer only, or also edge talking to multiple DISTINCT concentrators simultaneously (different from the existing Q18 single-active-hub failover)? Recommend concentrator-only for this goal; edge-multi-hub is a separate feature.
- PSK model: per-peer PSK (recommended, enables authenticated demux) vs keep deployment-wide PSK + some other demux? What's the migration/back-compat story for existing single-peer configs (top-level psk)?
- Demux bootstrapping: how is a path attributed to a peer for the very FIRST frames, before any authenticated PROBE / before the WG handshake completes? (provisional/quarantine resequencer? gate DATA until an authenticated PROBE binds the path?)
- Roaming: when an edge's source rebinds (NAT), how does the path re-bind to the same peer without a window where frames misroute?
- Resource limits: max peers per concentrator; per-peer memory (a ~2048-frame resequencer ring + FEC state each); backpressure / eviction of idle peers; is there a configured cap?
- Security: can a malicious edge (that knows ITS psk) disrupt ANOTHER edge's tunnel? Threat-model the path->peer binding.
- Metrics: per-peer label on the /metrics series (wanbond_path_* etc.).

NON-GOALS: mesh/anycast; edge-side simultaneous multi-concentrator aggregation; changing the single-engine-per-process model; any DPI/obfuscation change.

TESTING DIRECTION: netns e2e with 2+ edges to one concentrator proving per-peer isolation (each edge's traffic resequences independently; one edge's loss/reorder/restart does not corrupt another's stream; return traffic routes to the correct edge); a per-peer resequencer unit test (two interleaved outer-seq streams stay separated); realhosts extension if feasible. Report-only real-link where absolute numbers apply, per the existing M10/Q12 discipline.","sessionLogs":[".cq/logs/20260713-210054-acde8de5f9cf22718.md",".cq/logs/20260713-223042-a2b09d2ae330ca1d8.md",".cq/logs/20260713-223042-a2c165322dcecdcd0.md",".cq/logs/20260713-223626-a79ddfeb2486cc79e.md",".cq/logs/20260713-224024-ac5e7e9c498fab1f8.md"],"rawLogs":[".cq/logs/raw/20260713-210054-acde8de5f9cf22718.jsonl",".cq/logs/raw/20260713-223042-a2b09d2ae330ca1d8.jsonl",".cq/logs/raw/20260713-223042-a2c165322dcecdcd0.jsonl",".cq/logs/raw/20260713-223626-a79ddfeb2486cc79e.jsonl",".cq/logs/raw/20260713-224024-ac5e7e9c498fab1f8.jsonl"],"milestones":["M23","M24","M25","M26","M27"],"grounding":"Synthesized from 2 configured candidate planners (opus, fable; generate-N-then-judge per Q100/Q101; base = opus's 5-milestone/20-task candidate for its finer per-task testability, D32-per-peer isolation task, and the FEC prefix-stability invariant re-check the goal mandates; folded in from fable: the explicit pathState split into SHARED socket state vs per-(peer,path) codec/remote/prober/tx/rx — concentrator sockets are shared across peers — plus edge-role-with->1-peer rejection (Q21), uapiConfig golden byte-identity acceptance, (peer,path)-keyed metrics rate maps, and lazy per-peer state instantiation). The config schema and the WireGuard engine already accept multiple [[wireguard.peers]] (config.validate only requires >=1; device.go uapiConfig ranges over all peers), but wanbond's multipath Bind is a single-tunnel bonding instance: internal/bind/multipath.go holds ONE virtual endpoint (m.virt, pinned to the first source it learns), ONE receive resequencer (m.resequencer, keyed purely by outer-seq with no peer separation), ONE send counter (m.outerSeq), ONE scheduler, ONE fecSend/fecRecv, ONE flat m.paths list, and ONE defaultRemote. So with two edges, their independent outer-seq spaces interleave into one resequencer release window (D32-class drops), the single virtual endpoint + per-path endpoint-learning clobber, and return traffic misroutes. Design rule A1 already SAYS 'one virtual endpoint per peer' — the code took the single-peer shortcut. The plural [[wireguard.peers]] schema is therefore misleading and should either work or be documented as unsupported.

CHOSEN DIRECTION (to confirm/refine in planning): approach (2) — AUTHENTICATED path->peer binding, NOT a forgeable outer-frame tunnel id. The crux is that the Bind demuxes frames BELOW the crypto layer (it resequences/FEC-recovers before the engine decrypts), so it cannot use the engine's Noise peer identity, and DATA frames carry no peer id (frame.go: OuterSeq/PathID/FEC only) and are unauthenticated by design (invariant 4) — an outer tunnel id would be spoofable across edges (cross-peer resequencer-injection DoS). The recommended enabler is a PER-PEER PSK (move psk from top-level into [[wireguard.peers]]), so the PSK-HMAC PROBE/CONTROL plane authenticates the SPECIFIC peer and can establish a path->peer binding; DATA frames on that path then route to that peer's resequencer. Then de-singleton the Bind: make virt / resequencer / outerSeq / scheduler / fecSend / fecRecv / paths / defaultRemote / probers / reflector per-peer (a map[peer]peerState), with the engine-facing ReceiveFunc demuxing each frame to the right peerState and Send(bufs, endpoint) routing via an endpoint->peerState map.

KNOWN REFACTOR SURFACE: internal/bind/multipath.go (the de-singleton + demux), internal/bind/bind.go (keep the conn-seam isolation), internal/reseq (per-peer instances), internal/sched + internal/fec + internal/telemetry (per-peer), internal/frame (only if a wire change proves necessary), internal/device/device.go (program the path->peer demux table from authenticated peer events; per-peer virtual endpoints to the engine), internal/config (per-peer psk + validation).

INVARIANTS TO PRESERVE (do not break): A1 one-virtual-endpoint-per-peer (now literally per-peer); own outer-seq space per sender (never the inner WG counter); resequence before inner anti-replay; DATA/PARITY unauthenticated by design (do not add a forgeable demux that weakens this); PROBE/CONTROL PSK-HMAC authenticated with monotonic anti-replay; conn coupling stays isolated to internal/bind/bind.go; amnezia all-or-nothing + single-engine-per-process; reedsolomon prefix-stability (TestKlauspostParityPrefixStableInvariant) on any FEC touch.

- Scope: concentrator-side multi-peer only, or also edge talking to multiple DISTINCT concentrators simultaneously (different from the existing Q18 single-active-hub failover)? Recommend concentrator-only for this goal; edge-multi-hub is a separate feature.
- PSK model: per-peer PSK (recommended, enables authenticated demux) vs keep deployment-wide PSK + some other demux? What's the migration/back-compat story for existing single-peer configs (top-level psk)?
- Demux bootstrapping: how is a path attributed to a peer for the very FIRST frames, before any authenticated PROBE / before the WG handshake completes? (provisional/quarantine resequencer? gate DATA until an authenticated PROBE binds the path?)
- Roaming: when an edge's source rebinds (NAT), how does the path re-bind to the same peer without a window where frames misroute?
- Resource limits: max peers per concentrator; per-peer memory (a ~2048-frame resequencer ring + FEC state each); backpressure / eviction of idle peers; is there a configured cap?
- Security: can a malicious edge (that knows ITS psk) disrupt ANOTHER edge's tunnel? Threat-model the path->peer binding.
- Metrics: per-peer label on the /metrics series (wanbond_path_* etc.).

NON-GOALS: mesh/anycast; edge-side simultaneous multi-concentrator aggregation; changing the single-engine-per-process model; any DPI/obfuscation change.

// collector is a prometheus.Collector that reads a Source at scrape time and
// emits per-path const-metrics plus the placeholder FEC counters. Reading at
// scrape time (rather than mirroring into GaugeVecs on an update path) keeps the
// exposition consistent with the live telemetry with no duplicated state and no
// staleness window.
type collector struct {
	src Source

	txBytes    *prometheus.Desc
	rxBytes    *prometheus.Desc
	loss       *prometheus.Desc
	rtt        *prometheus.Desc
	jitter     *prometheus.Desc
	throughput *prometheus.Desc
	up         *prometheus.Desc

	fecData          *prometheus.Desc
	fecRepair        *prometheus.Desc
	fecRecovered     *prometheus.Desc
	fecUnrecoverable *prometheus.Desc
	fecDataBytes     *prometheus.Desc
	fecRepairBytes   *prometheus.Desc
	fecResidualLoss  *prometheus.Desc

	sessionEstablished   *prometheus.Desc
	sessionLastHandshake *prometheus.Desc
}

// NewCollector builds the wanbond metrics collector over src. Register it into a
// dedicated prometheus.Registry (see NewServer); it deliberately does not touch
// the global default registry (no-globals discipline).
func NewCollector(src Source) prometheus.Collector {
	pathLabels := []string{labelPath}
	desc := func(subsystem, name, help string, labels []string) *prometheus.Desc {
		return prometheus.NewDesc(prometheus.BuildFQName(namespace, subsystem, name), help, labels, nil)
	}
	return &collector{
		src:        src,
		txBytes:    desc(pathSubsystem, "tx_bytes_total", "Total bytes transmitted on the path.", pathLabels),
		rxBytes:    desc(pathSubsystem, "rx_bytes_total", "Total bytes received on the path.", pathLabels),
		loss:       desc(pathSubsystem, "loss_ratio", "Per-path probe loss fraction in [0,1].", pathLabels),
		rtt:        desc(pathSubsystem, "rtt_seconds", "Smoothed per-path round-trip time in seconds.", pathLabels),
		jitter:     desc(pathSubsystem, "jitter_seconds", "Smoothed per-path RTT deviation (jitter) in seconds.", pathLabels),
		throughput: desc(pathSubsystem, "throughput_bits_per_second", "Current per-path throughput in bits per second.", pathLabels),
		up:         desc(pathSubsystem, "up", "Per-path liveness (1 = up, 0 = down).", pathLabels),

		fecData:          desc(fecSubsystem, "data_packets_total", "FEC DATA packets emitted (the fixed-ratio overhead denominator).", nil),
		fecRepair:        desc(fecSubsystem, "repair_packets_total", "FEC parity packets emitted (the fixed-ratio overhead).", nil),
		fecRecovered:     desc(fecSubsystem, "recovered_packets_total", "Data packets reconstructed via FEC.", nil),
		fecUnrecoverable: desc(fecSubsystem, "unrecoverable_packets_total", "Data packets lost beyond FEC repair capacity.", nil),
		fecDataBytes:     desc(fecSubsystem, "data_bytes_total", "FEC DATA-frame wire bytes emitted (the byte overhead denominator).", nil),
		fecRepairBytes:   desc(fecSubsystem, "repair_bytes_total", "FEC parity-frame wire bytes emitted (the byte overhead numerator).", nil),
		fecResidualLoss:  desc(fecSubsystem, "residual_loss_ratio", "Post-FEC-recovery connection loss fraction in [0,1] (loss FEC did not mask).", nil),

		sessionEstablished:   desc(sessionSubsystem, "established", "WG session liveness (1 = a handshake has completed and is still fresh, 0 = still converging or wedged).", nil),
		sessionLastHandshake: desc(sessionSubsystem, "last_handshake_seconds", "Age in seconds of the peer's most recent completed WG handshake (0 when none has completed).", nil),
	}
}

// Describe sends every descriptor; the collector's series set is fixed even
// though its per-path label values are discovered at Collect time.
func (c *collector) Describe(ch chan<- *prometheus.Desc) {
	ch <- c.txBytes
	ch <- c.rxBytes
	ch <- c.loss
	ch <- c.rtt
	ch <- c.jitter
	ch <- c.throughput
	ch <- c.up
	ch <- c.fecData
	ch <- c.fecRepair
	ch <- c.fecRecovered
	ch <- c.fecUnrecoverable
	ch <- c.fecDataBytes
	ch <- c.fecRepairBytes
	ch <- c.fecResidualLoss
	ch <- c.sessionEstablished
	ch <- c.sessionLastHandshake
}

// Collect reads the Source once and emits one const-metric per per-path series,
// then the three connection-scoped FEC counters read from the live FEC plane.
func (c *collector) Collect(ch chan<- prometheus.Metric) {
	for _, p := range c.src.Paths() {
		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.txBytes, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(p.TxBytes), p.Name)
		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.rxBytes, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(p.RxBytes), p.Name)
		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.loss, prometheus.GaugeValue, p.Estimate.Loss, p.Name)
		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.rtt, prometheus.GaugeValue, p.Estimate.RTT.Seconds(), p.Name)
		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.jitter, prometheus.GaugeValue, p.Estimate.Jitter.Seconds(), p.Name)
		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.throughput, prometheus.GaugeValue, p.ThroughputBitsPerSecond, p.Name)
		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.up, prometheus.GaugeValue, upValue(p.State), p.Name)
	}
	f := c.src.FEC()
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecData, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.DataPackets))
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecRepair, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.RepairPackets))
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecRecovered, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.RecoveredPackets))
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecUnrecoverable, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.UnrecoverablePackets))
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecDataBytes, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.DataBytes))
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecRepairBytes, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.RepairBytes))
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecResidualLoss, prometheus.GaugeValue, f.ResidualLossRatio)

	sess := c.src.Session()
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.sessionEstablished, prometheus.GaugeValue, establishedValue(sess.Established))
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.sessionLastHandshake, prometheus.GaugeValue, sess.LastHandshakeAge.Seconds())
}

		fecDataBytes:     desc(fecSubsystem, "data_bytes_total", "FEC DATA-frame wire bytes emitted (the byte overhead denominator).", nil),
		fecRepairBytes:   desc(fecSubsystem, "repair_bytes_total", "FEC parity-frame wire bytes emitted (the byte overhead numerator).", nil),
		fecResidualLoss:  desc(fecSubsystem, "residual_loss_ratio", "Post-FEC-recovery connection loss fraction in [0,1] (loss FEC did not mask).", nil),

		sessionEstablished:   desc(sessionSubsystem, "established", "WG session liveness (1 = a handshake has completed and is still fresh, 0 = still converging or wedged).", nil),
		sessionLastHandshake: desc(sessionSubsystem, "last_handshake_seconds", "Age in seconds of the peer's most recent completed WG handshake (0 when none has completed).", nil),
	}
}

// Describe sends every descriptor; the collector's series set is fixed even
// though its per-path label values are discovered at Collect time.
func (c *collector) Describe(ch chan<- *prometheus.Desc) {
	ch <- c.txBytes
	ch <- c.rxBytes
	ch <- c.loss
	ch <- c.rtt
	ch <- c.jitter
	ch <- c.throughput
	ch <- c.up
	ch <- c.fecData
	ch <- c.fecRepair
	ch <- c.fecRecovered
	ch <- c.fecUnrecoverable
	ch <- c.fecDataBytes
	ch <- c.fecRepairBytes
	ch <- c.fecResidualLoss
	ch <- c.sessionEstablished
	ch <- c.sessionLastHandshake
}

// Collect reads the Source once and emits one const-metric per per-path series,
// then the three connection-scoped FEC counters read from the live FEC plane.
func (c *collector) Collect(ch chan<- prometheus.Metric) {
	for _, p := range c.src.Paths() {
		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.txBytes, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(p.TxBytes), p.Name)
		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.rxBytes, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(p.RxBytes), p.Name)
		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.loss, prometheus.GaugeValue, p.Estimate.Loss, p.Name)
		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.rtt, prometheus.GaugeValue, p.Estimate.RTT.Seconds(), p.Name)
		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.jitter, prometheus.GaugeValue, p.Estimate.Jitter.Seconds(), p.Name)
		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.throughput, prometheus.GaugeValue, p.ThroughputBitsPerSecond, p.Name)
		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.up, prometheus.GaugeValue, upValue(p.State), p.Name)
	}
	f := c.src.FEC()
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecData, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.DataPackets))
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecRepair, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.RepairPackets))
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecRecovered, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.RecoveredPackets))
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecUnrecoverable, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.UnrecoverablePackets))
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecDataBytes, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.DataBytes))
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecRepairBytes, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.RepairBytes))
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecResidualLoss, prometheus.GaugeValue, f.ResidualLossRatio)

	sess := c.src.Session()
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.sessionEstablished, prometheus.GaugeValue, establishedValue(sess.Established))
	ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.sessionLastHandshake, prometheus.GaugeValue, sess.LastHandshakeAge.Seconds())
}

## Operating it

  adds/removes paths without tearing the tunnel down.
- **Metrics**: set `[metrics].listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"` (loopback only — a
  non-loopback bind is refused) and scrape `/metrics` for per-path loss, FEC
  recovery, throughput, and probed RTT/liveness.
- **Logs**: structured, to stderr → `journalctl -u wanbond-…`.

### Series to watch

Exact exported series (labels/help verbatim from `internal/metrics/metrics.go`).
Every per-path series carries a `path="<name>"` label matching the `[[paths]]`
`name`:

| Series                                          | Meaning / what to watch |
|-------------------------------------------------|-------------------------|
| `wanbond_path_up{path}`                         | Per-path liveness, `1`=up `0`=down. **Primary health signal.** |
| `wanbond_path_throughput_bits_per_second{path}` | Current per-path throughput; confirms traffic is striping. |
| `wanbond_path_loss_ratio{path}`                 | Probe loss fraction `[0,1]`; rising loss precedes a path drop. |
| `wanbond_path_rtt_seconds{path}`                | Smoothed RTT; a jump signals congestion/bufferbloat. |
| `wanbond_path_jitter_seconds{path}`             | Smoothed RTT deviation. |
| `wanbond_path_tx_bytes_total{path}` / `wanbond_path_rx_bytes_total{path}` | Per-path byte counters. |
| `wanbond_fec_recovered_packets_total`           | DATA packets reconstructed by FEC (masked loss). |
| `wanbond_fec_unrecoverable_packets_total`       | DATA lost beyond FEC repair — **should stay near-flat**. |
